questionText: A ball is thrown so that, $t$ seconds after release, its position in metres is modelled by $x = 8t$ and $y = 30t - 5t^{2}$. Find the greatest height reached, the horizontal distance travelled at that instant, and the speed of the ball at the top of its flight.

steps.description: Differentiate the height with respect to t
steps.explanation: The vertical velocity $\dot{y}$ tells us how fast the ball is rising. At the very top it is momentarily neither rising nor falling.

steps.description: Set the vertical velocity to zero
steps.explanation: The greatest height happens exactly when $\dot{y} = 0$ — the instant the upward motion stops before the ball begins to fall.
This is the time in seconds at which the ball is at the top of its path.
The vertical velocity changes from positive to negative, so the ball rises then falls: $t = 3$ really is the highest point, not a lowest one.

steps.description: Substitute the time into the height
steps.explanation: Putting the time back into $y$ gives the actual height at that instant, which is the greatest height.

steps.description: Find the horizontal distance at that time
steps.explanation: The same time is used in the horizontal equation; there is no need to differentiate $x$ because we only want where the ball is.
The horizontal velocity is constant at $8$ m/s and is needed for the speed at the top.

steps.description: Use the speed formula at the top
steps.explanation: Speed is the length of the velocity vector. At the top $\dot{y} = 0$, so only the horizontal component remains.
At the highest point the ball moves purely horizontally, so its speed there is just the horizontal velocity, $8$ m/s.
Collecting the three answers gives the full description of the ball at the top of its flight.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The greatest height is $45$ m, reached after travelling $24$ m horizontally (at $t = 3$ s), where the ball's speed is $8$ m/s.
